hints: In the 2014 Adelaide Test, he became the first Indian to score two hundreds in his debut match as captain.
Holds the record for most double centuries as a Test captain (7), surpassing Brian Lara.
The only Indian captain to lead the side to a Test series victory on Australian soil (2018-19).
Bowled the 'Ball of the Century' to dismiss Mike Gatting at Old Trafford in 1993 with his first delivery in Ashes cricket.
The first player in history to reach 700 Test wickets, achieving the feat at his home ground, the MCG, in 2006.
Holds the record for the most Test runs (3154) scored in a career without ever making a century.
Holds the world record for the most catches taken by a non-wicketkeeper in Test history (210 catches).
The only player to have been involved in two 300-plus run partnerships in the Cricket World Cup, but in Tests, he has faced more deliveries (31,258) than anyone else.
Famously batted for 835 minutes to score 270 against Pakistan in Rawalpindi (2004), leading India to its first series win there.
Holds the world record for scoring the most runs in a single over in Test cricket, hitting Stuart Broad for 35 runs in 2022.
The first Asian fast bowler to take a five-wicket haul in South Africa, England, and Australia in the same calendar year (2018).
Reached 150 Test wickets in just 6,781 balls, the fastest for any Indian bowler in terms of balls bowled.
The only player to score centuries in both innings of his debut Test as an opening batsman (vs South Africa, 2019).
Holds the record for the most sixes in a single Test match, clearing the boundary 13 times against South Africa in Vizag.
Started his Test career with two consecutive centuries (177 and 111*) against the West Indies in Sachin Tendulkar's farewell series.
A man who spends more time analyzing the physics of a cricket ball than actually sleeping.
The only person in the world who thinks Mankading is a form of high art rather than a cardinal sin.
A scientist disguised as an off-spinner who has a different variation for every day of the year and a YouTube channel to explain why you're wrong.
The first player to be given out by a third umpire in international cricket (1992 vs South Africa).
Dismissed Moin Khan with a 'magic' leg-break on the last ball of the day during the Multan Test in 2004.
The only cricketer to play 200 Test matches and score 100 international centuries.
Reached his first triple century (309) at Multan by hitting Saqlain Mushtaq for a six while batting on 295.
One of only four batsmen to score two triple centuries in Test cricket, alongside Don Bradman, Brian Lara, and Chris Gayle.
Holds the record for the fastest triple century in Test history, reaching 300 in just 278 balls against South Africa at Chennai.
Became only the second bowler in history, after Jim Laker, to take all 10 wickets in a Test innings (vs Pakistan at Feroz Shah Kotla, 1999).
Batted with a broken jaw for 14 overs and dismissed Brian Lara in the Antigua Test of 2002.
India's highest wicket-taker in Tests and the only Indian bowler to deliver over 40,000 balls in the format.
The fastest left-arm bowler to reach 200 Test wickets, achieving the feat in 44 matches.
The first Indian left-arm spinner to take 300 Test wickets, joining the elite 300-wicket/3000-run all-rounder club.
Scored three triple centuries in First-Class cricket (Ranji Trophy) before establishing himself as a Test mainstay.
He never missed a single Test match in his career due to fitness or injury over 131 appearances.
Famously hit Eddie Hemmings for four consecutive sixes in the Lord's Test (1990) to avoid the follow-on.
Held the world record for the most Test wickets (434) after surpassing Richard Hadlee in 1994.
The first player in Test history to aggregate 1,000 runs in a calendar year on four separate occasions.
The first batsman to reach the milestone of 10,000 Test runs, achieving it against Pakistan in Ahmedabad.
Held the record for the most Test centuries (34) for over two decades before it was broken in 2005.
The first Indian bowler to take a hat-trick in Test cricket, achieving it during the 2001 Eden Gardens Test.
Nicknamed 'The Turbanator', he famously dismissed Ricky Ponting 10 times in Test matches.
Is one of the few specialist bowlers to score back-to-back Test centuries, doing so against New Zealand in 2010.
The only wicketkeeper-captain to lead his team in 60 Test matches.
Scored a blistering 224 against Australia in Chennai (2013), the highest score by an Indian wicketkeeper in Tests.
Known for his unorthodox keeping, he has the most stumpings in international cricket across all formats.
The only player in Test history to be involved in 100 Test victories.
Scored twin centuries (120 & 143*) in his 100th Test match against South Africa at Sydney.
Australia's leading run-scorer in both Test and ODI cricket.
Holds the record for playing the most consecutive Tests (98) from debut without being dropped.
In 2012 against Australia, he batted for over four hours to score 33 off 220 balls to save a Test match.
Known as 'Mr. 360', he once held the record for the fastest Test century by a South African (75 balls).
The first bowler to introduce the 'knuckle ball' on a world stage during the 2011 World Cup.
Dismissed South African captain Graeme Smith 11 times in international cricket, including 7 times in Tests.
Held the record for the highest score by a No. 11 batsman in Test history (75 runs vs Australia) for nearly a decade.
Had eyes that looked like they were popping out in horror every time he unleashed a ball that turned 90 degrees.
Proved his action was legal so many times that the ICC basically had to change the laws just to stop people from crying about it.
A smiling assassin who took 800 wickets and still looks like he’s just happy to be invited to the party.
The first Indian wicketkeeper to score Test centuries in England, Australia, and South Africa.
Famously hit the winning runs at the Gabba in 2021, ending Australia's 32-year unbeaten streak at the venue.
Holds the record for the fastest Test century by an Indian wicketkeeper (89 balls vs England, 2022).
The most handsome man in cricket who also happens to be a ruthless assassin with a red ball in his hand.
Captained Australia to a World Test Championship and an Ashes win while looking like he's just finished a photoshoot for a luxury watch brand.
Spent six years in the injury wilderness and returned as a biological anomaly who never seems to bowl a bad delivery or have a bad hair day.
His 281 at Eden Gardens in 2001 was voted the greatest Test performance of the last 50 years by Wisden.
A specialist at batting with the tail, he famously guided India to a 1-wicket win over Australia at Mohali in 2010 while battling back spasms.
The only player to score over 2000 Test runs against Australia without ever playing in a World Cup.
Reclaimed the record for the highest individual Test score by hitting 400 not out against England in 2004.
Scored 28 runs in a single over off Robin Peterson in 2003, which was a world record for 18 years.
The only batsman to score a quintuple century (501*) in first-class history and a quadruple century in Tests.
One of only four players to score two triple centuries in Test cricket.
The first player in Test history to hit a six off the very first ball of a match (vs Bangladesh, 2012).
Known as the 'Universe Boss', he carried his bat through an entire Test innings against Australia in 2009 for 165*.
As tall and narrow as a lamppost and just as fun to run into when you're trying to score runs.
Had a strike rate of approximately one 'I told you so' per wicket, famously predicting 5-0 whitewashes before every series.
Nicknamed 'Pigeon', he spent fifteen years hitting a 50-paise coin on a good length until the batsman eventually died of frustration.
He holds the record for the highest individual score by a Sri Lankan in Test cricket, 340 against India in 1997.
In the same innings where he scored 340, he shared a world-record partnership of 576 runs with Roshan Mahanama.
One of the few players to have scored a triple century and taken more than 90 wickets in Test matches.
Held the record for the most sixes in Test cricket (100) for several years until surpassed by Brendon McCullum.
Scored the then second-fastest century in Test history in just 57 balls against England at Perth in 2006.
Famously walked in the 2003 World Cup semi-final, but in Tests, he maintained a career strike rate of 81.95, revolutionary for a keeper.
Known as the 'Sultan of Swing', he was the first bowler to reach the 400-wicket mark in both Tests and ODIs.
Holds the record for the highest score by a number 8 batsman in Test history (257* against Zimbabwe).
Took two Test hat-tricks in back-to-back matches against Sri Lanka in 1999.
In his final 10 years of Test cricket, he played 51 Tests, averaging a remarkable 50 with the bat and 19 with the ball.
Led Pakistan to their first-ever Test series win in England in 1987.
Achieved the 'all-rounder's double' of 3000 runs and 300 wickets in just 75 Tests, the second fastest at the time.
Known for his unique round-arm action, often described as 'slinging'.
Took a hat-trick in his very first First-Class game, but in Tests, he famously retired early to preserve his fitness for white-ball cricket.
He dismissed 4 batsmen in 4 balls in an ODI, but in his brief Test career, he provided the vital breakthrough in Sri Lanka's first Test win in England.
The man chewed gum with more aggression than most modern openers show while actually batting.
Never wore a helmet because he genuinely believed no fast bowler was brave enough to actually hit him.
The ultimate 'Swagger' king who treated the best bowlers in the world like they were just there to facilitate his boundaries.
His first ball of an Ashes series once went so far down leg side it practically ended up in a different time zone.
The ultimate 'mismatch' bowler who treats tailenders like they personally insulted his dog.
A tall, lanky left-armer whose ankle is held together by sticky tape, prayers, and the hope of an early swing.
Grew his hair out like a Bollywood villain and spent 15 years bowling 'the heavy ball' without anyone knowing what that actually meant.
Famously made a face at Steve Smith that became a meme more iconic than half of his career wickets.
The man who bullied Ricky Ponting as a teenager in Perth and then spent the next decade trying to remember how he did it.
The man who thinks strike rates are a Western conspiracy to destroy the sanctity of a Tuesday afternoon session.
Took more hits to the body in Brisbane than a professional boxer just to make sure the bowlers ran out of hope.
A modern-day monk who bats like he has a flight to catch in three days and absolutely nowhere else to be until then.
The man who single-handedly ruined India's 2023 by scoring a century in both the WTC and the World Cup finals.
Possesses a mustache that makes him look like an extra from a 70s adult film and a strike rate that makes Test match purists weep.
He doesn't move his feet, he doesn't respect the new ball, and somehow he still ends up with a 100 before the lunch break.
Nicknamed the 'Rawalpindi Express', he is credited with bowling the fastest delivery in cricket history at 161.3 km/h.
Famously clean-bowled Rahul Dravid and Sachin Tendulkar on consecutive deliveries at Eden Gardens in 1999.
His career was plagued by controversies and injuries, yet he remained the first bowler to break the 100 mph barrier.
Scored a historic century at Lord's in 2014, helping India secure a famous win on a green top.
Led India as captain to a comeback series win in Australia (2020-21) after the team was bowled out for 36 in the first Test.
The first Indian fielder to take eight catches in a single Test match (vs Sri Lanka, 2015).
Batted for 607 minutes to score 196 against Australia in Karachi (2022), the highest score by a captain in the fourth innings of a Test.
Only player in the modern era to be ranked in the top 5 across all three formats simultaneously.
Reached 2000 Test runs in just 36 innings, faster than many contemporary greats.
Needed just four runs in his final Test innings to finish with a career average of 100, but was out for a duck.
Holds the record for the most double centuries in Test history (12) and the most runs in a single series (974).
Scored 300 runs in a single day's play during the Headingley Test of 1930.
The first Pakistani to win the Sir Garfield Sobers Trophy for ICC Men's Cricketer of the Year (2021).
At age 21, he took 10 wickets in a Test match against the West Indies at Kingston.
Known for his lethal opening spells, he consistently clocks over 145 kph with a high-arm left-over action.
The man who made Steve Waugh wait at the toss just to prove that the era of Indian politeness was officially dead.
Taking off his shirt at Lord's balcony was the most aggressive thing a Bengali man has done since the 1905 partition.
God on the off-side, but a total mortal against anything aimed at his ribcage or delivered by a guy who could actually run in fast.
The only batsman to score centuries in each of his first three Test matches.
Captain who led India to an unbeaten home record in the 1990s, winning 14 Tests without a single loss.
Scored a career-best 199 against Sri Lanka at Kanpur, famously dismissed one run short of a double ton.
Possesses a technique so aesthetically pleasing that coaches use it for demos, yet he spends half his innings looking like he's having an existential crisis.
The ultimate 'utility' victim who has batted everywhere from opener to wicketkeeper just because the team management doesn't know what to do with his talent.
He can score a century at Lord's and then disappear for three series, leaving fans wondering if he's a world-class batter or just a very stylish model.
Took 9 wickets on his Test debut against the West Indies in 2013, the most by an Indian pacer on debut.
Has a higher strike rate in the second innings of Tests, earning him the nickname 'Second Innings Shami'.

The only Indian to score centuries in five consecutive Test matches (2009-10).
Batted for nearly 11 hours (643 minutes) to score 137 in Napier to save a Test match against New Zealand.
Reached the ICC World No. 1 Test batsman ranking in 2009, the same year he won the ICC Test Player of the Year.
Known as the 'Mysore Express', he was India's fastest bowler in the 1990s with a recorded speed of 157 kph.
Took 8 wickets for 86 runs in the second innings against Pakistan at Kolkata in 1999.
Is the only Indian fast bowler to take over 200 wickets in both Tests and ODIs.
Worked as a groundsman at the Adelaide Oval before being fast-tracked into the Australian Test side.
Has taken the most Test wickets for an Australian off-spinner, surpassing Hugh Trumble.
Affectionately nicknamed 'Garry' or 'GOAT' by his teammates and the Australian public.
Scored 11 double centuries in Test cricket, a record second only to Sir Don Bradman.
Shared a 624-run stand with Mahela Jayawardene against South Africa, the highest partnership in first-class history.
Is the fastest player to reach the milestones of 8000, 9000, 10000, 11000, and 12000 Test runs.
Made his debut as a leg spinner and batting at No 8
Barmy army's 'We saw you cry on the telly' made little difference on his comeback tour. Made 144, 142, 92, 211, 82 in that series
Australia's best test batsman after Don Bradman.
The only batsman to score Test centuries in all 11 countries that have hosted Test matches.
The highest run-scorer and most prolific century-maker for Pakistan in Test cricket history.
One of the few players to score a Test triple century and a century in his 100th Test match.

The first and only pace bowler to reach the milestone of 700 Test wickets.

The first captain to lead a team to 16 consecutive Test victories, a streak ended by India at Eden Gardens in 2001.
One of only a few players to score over 10,000 Test runs and take over 90 wickets in the format.
Has the face of a 12-year-old choir boy but the back of an 80-year-old man from carrying his entire batting lineup for a decade.
Famously got punched in a bar by David Warner, which is probably the most interesting thing that has ever happened to him off the field.
The king of the 'daddy hundred' who still hasn't figured out how to win a series in Australia despite playing there since the stone age.
The most boringly efficient cricketer to ever live; he played like a high-functioning robot programmed by a very dry accountant.
Had a hair transplant that was more successful than most people's entire careers.
The human equivalent of a brick wall that could also bowl 140 clicks, yet somehow managed to be the least charismatic legend in the hall of fame.
The first Australian cricketer in 132 years to be selected for a national team in any format without playing first-class cricket.
Smashed 335* against Pakistan at Adelaide in 2019, breaking Mark Taylor and Don Bradman's record for the highest score at the venue.
Scored a century in his 100th Test match against South Africa, becoming only the second Australian to do so after Ricky Ponting.
Holds the record for the fastest Test double century by an Englishman, reaching the milestone in 163 balls against South Africa.

Despite his legendary batting, he is statistically one of the most frequently run-out players in international history.
The man is so annoyingly nice that he probably apologizes to the wicketkeeper every time he accidentally leaves a ball alone.
Bats like he's trying to save a failing marriage—total commitment, zero flashiness, and an incredible amount of patience.
Possesses a technique so tight that a single mistake from him feels like a glitch in the simulation of New Zealand's greatest ever run-scorer.
Took a wicket with his fourth delivery in Test cricket against India at Melbourne in 1999.
One of the fastest bowlers in history, consistently clocking over 150 kph and recording a top speed of 161.1 kph.
The first player to take a hat-trick in T20 international cricket, though he finished with 310 Test wickets.
Had a vein in his forehead that looked like it was going to explode every time he took a wicket or saw a batsman survive a bouncer.
The 'Steyn Gun' who could make a red ball reverse swing at 150 clicks like it was being guided by a heat-seeking laser.
Possessed a celebration that looked like he was trying to summon a demon, which is fair considering how he bowled.
Pioneered the art of reverse swing at high pace, famously known for his lethal toe-crushing yorkers.
The youngest-ever Test captain for Pakistan, appointed at the age of 22 years and 15 days.
Holds one of the best strike rates in Test history for any bowler with more than 200 wickets.
The first Indian to hit six sixes in an over in first-class cricket, achieving the feat for Mumbai against Baroda.
Started his career as a number 10 batsman but eventually opened the innings, scoring a double century in Australia.

An elegant left-hander who scored back-to-back centuries in his first two Test matches against Sri Lanka.

The first player from Vidarbha to represent India in Test cricket.

Known as 'Sixer Sidhu' for his ability to charge down the track and hit spinners out of the park.

A man whose ego was so large it needed its own passport and a separate locker room.

The only England player brilliant enough to dismantle prime Australia and annoying enough to get sacked via a group text message.

The man was so grumpy they literally nicknamed him 'Captain Grumpy' while he dragged a mediocre team into relevance.

The original architect of the Australian 'ugly win' who would rather bat for ten hours than smile for ten seconds.

Nicknamed 'The Naenae Express', he was the perfect foil to Richard Hadlee, known for his relentless accuracy and nagging line.
Survived a near-fatal incident on debut in 1975 when he was hit on the head by a bouncer and his heart stopped, requiring mouth-to-mouth resuscitation.
Maintained an economy rate of just 2.05 over 43 Tests, making him one of the most difficult bowlers to score against in the 1980s.
A pillar of South African batting for two decades, he famously scored two centuries in a Test match against England at The Oval in 1947.
Known for his incredible concentration, he carried his bat through a Test innings against England at Lord's in 1947, scoring 120*.
Held the record for the most Test runs for South Africa for several decades and was also a very capable leg-break bowler.
A workhorse medium-pacer who provided vital support to the emerging West Indian 'pace quartet' in the early to mid-1970s.
Took a career-best 6 for 28 against India at Bridgetown in 1975 to help secure a dominant series win.
Known for his impeccable seam movement and stamina, he later became an international umpire after his playing days.
New Zealand's leading wicket-taker at the time of his retirement, he was a tall left-arm pacer who extracted steep bounce.
Famously scored 68* as a number 11 against Pakistan, which stood as a world record for the highest score in that position for 30 years.
Played a major role in New Zealand's first-ever Test victory over England in 1978, taking match figures of 6 for 112.
One of the original 'Four Horsemen of the Apocalypse', known for his aggressive, slinging action and bowling from very wide of the crease.
Recorded the best-ever bowling figures by a West Indian fast bowler in an innings, taking 8 for 29 against Pakistan in 1977.
His international career was cut short when he joined the controversial rebel tour to South Africa in 1982.
Nicknamed 'Garth' due to his muscular build, he was the youngest Australian to take 100 Test wickets at the time.
The spearhead of the Australian attack in the 1960s, he took 246 Test wickets and was known for his extreme durability and pace.
Famously took 8 for 71 against the West Indies at Perth in 1968, terrorizing a lineup that included Sobers and Kanhai.
Captained New Zealand in 34 Test matches and led them to their first three Test victories in national history.
A powerful all-rounder who held the record for the most sixes in a first-class innings (15) for 35 years.
Was the only world-class player in the New Zealand side for much of the 1950s, often topping both the batting and bowling charts.
The first Black cricketer to be appointed captain of the West Indies for an entire series, leading the team during the famous 'Tied Test' tour.
One of the legendary 'Three Ws' of Barbadian cricket, he was knighted for his services to the game and his role in racial diplomacy.
He remains the only player to have been involved in two 500-run partnerships in first-class cricket.
One half of the 'spin twins' (with Sonny Ramadhin) who inspired the first West Indies victory in England in 1950.
Took 33 wickets in his debut Test series against England, a performance immortalized in the 'Victory Calypso'.
The first West Indian bowler to reach 100 Test wickets, known for his incredible stamina and huge revolutions on the ball.
The only player in Test history to score a century and take a five-wicket haul on debut (vs India at Kolkata, 1965).
Smashed the fastest Test century by a New Zealander at the time, reaching 100 in just 86 balls against the West Indies in 1969.
A dynamic all-rounder who remains a unique figure in Kiwi cricket history for his explosive debut and record-breaking hitting.
